turns.en: Okay, today I have the pleasure of interviewing Ilya Sutskever, who is the co-founder and chief scientist of OpenAI. Ilya, welcome to the Lunar Society.
turns.zh: 好的，今天我有幸采访到 OpenAI 的联合创始人兼首席科学家 Ilya Sutskever。Ilya，欢迎来到月球协会。

turns.en: Thank you, happy to be here.
turns.zh: 谢谢，很高兴来到这里。

turns.en: First question, and no humility allowed. There are many scientists who will make a big breakthrough in their field. There are far fewer scientists who will make multiple independent breakthroughs that define their field throughout their career. What is the difference? What distinguishes you from other researchers? Why have you been able to find multiple breakthroughs in a field?
turns.zh: 第一个问题，不许谦虚。很多科学家会在自己的领域取得重大突破，但能在整个职业生涯中做出多个独立突破、定义整个领域的科学家要少得多。区别是什么？是什么让你与其他研究者不同？为什么你能在一个领域取得多个突破？

turns.en: Well, thank you for the kind words. It's hard to answer that question. I mean, I try really hard. I gave it everything I got, and that worked so far. I think that's all there is to it.
turns.zh: 谢谢你的美言。这个问题很难回答。我的意思是，我非常努力，全力以赴，到目前为止这很有效。我想就是这样。

turns.en: What's the explanation for why there aren't more illicit uses of GPT? Why aren't more foreign governments using it to spread propaganda or scam grandmothers or something?
turns.zh: 为什么 GPT 没有被更广泛地非法使用？为什么更多外国政府没有用它来传播宣传或诈骗老奶奶之类的事情？

turns.en: I mean, maybe they haven't really gotten to do it a lot, but it also wouldn't surprise me if some of it was going on right now. Certainly, I imagine they'd be taking some of the open source models and trying to use them for that purpose. I would expect this would be something that'd be interested in in the future. It's technically possible; they just haven't thought about it enough or haven't done it at scale using their technology. Or maybe it's happening but you don't know.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，也许他们还没有真正大规模地做，但如果现在就有一些正在发生，我也不会感到惊讶。当然，我想他们会拿一些开源模型尝试用于这个目的。我预计未来这会成为他们感兴趣的事情。技术上可行，他们只是还没想够，或者还没有用他们的技术大规模实施。或者也许正在发生，但你不知道。

turns.en: Would you be able to track it if it was happening?
turns.zh: 如果正在发生，你能追踪到吗？

turns.en: I think large-scale tracking is possible, yes. I mean, this requires a special operation, but it is possible.
turns.zh: 我认为大规模追踪是可能的，是的。我的意思是，这需要特殊操作，但可行。

turns.en: Now there's some window in which AI is very economically valuable, on the scale of airplanes let's say, but we haven't reached AGI yet. How big is that window?
turns.zh: 现在有一个窗口期，AI 具有很高的经济价值，比如说像飞机那样，但我们还没有达到 AGI。这个窗口有多大？

turns.en: I think this window—it's hard to give you a precise answer, but it's definitely going to be a good multi-year window. It's also a question of definition, because AI before it becomes AGI is going to be increasingly more valuable year after year, I'd say in an exponential way. So in some sense, it may feel like, especially in hindsight, it may feel like there was only one year or two years because those two years were larger than the previous years. But I would say that already last year there has been a fair amount of economic value produced by AI, and next year it's going to be larger and larger after that. So I think it's going to be a good multi-year chunk of time, but that's going to be true from now to AGI pretty much.
turns.zh: 我认为这个窗口——很难给你一个精确的答案，但肯定是一个好几年的窗口。这也是一个定义问题，因为 AI 在成为 AGI 之前，其价值会逐年增加，而且是指数级的。所以在某种意义上，尤其是事后看来，可能感觉只有一两年，因为那两年的价值比之前所有年份都大。但我要说，去年 AI 已经产生了相当可观的经济价值，明年会更大，之后越来越大。所以我认为这会是一个好几年的时间段，而且从现在到 AGI 基本都会如此。

turns.en: I'm curious if there's a startup that's using your models right now. At some point, if you have AGI, there's only one business in the world, right? It's OpenAI. How much window do they have? Do any businesses have where they're actually producing something that AGI can't produce?
turns.zh: 我很好奇，如果现在有家创业公司在用你们的模型。到某个时候，如果你有了 AGI，世界上就只有一家企业了，对吧？就是 OpenAI。他们还有多少窗口期？有没有企业能真正生产出 AGI 无法生产的东西？

turns.en: Well, I mean, it's the same question as asking how long until AGI. I think it's a hard question to answer. I hesitate to give you a number, also because there is the St. Ives effect where people who are optimistic, people who are working on the technology, tend to underestimate the time it takes to get there. But I think the way I ground myself is by thinking about the self-driving car. In particular, there is an analogy: if you look at a Tesla and if you look at the self-driving behavior of it, it looks like it does everything all right, but it's also clear that there is still a long way to go in terms of reliability. And we might be in a similar place with respect to our models, where it also looks like we can do everything, and at the same time we'll need to do some more work until we really iron out all the issues and make it really good and really reliable and robust and well-behaved.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，这跟问多久能达到 AGI 是同一个问题。我觉得很难回答。我不太想给你一个数字，也是因为圣艾夫斯效应：那些乐观的、正在研究这项技术的人往往低估了达到目标所需的时间。但我让自己脚踏实地的方法是思考自动驾驶汽车。特别是有个类比：如果你看一辆特斯拉，看它的自动驾驶行为，它看起来什么都能做，但很明显在可靠性方面还有很长的路要走。我们的模型可能也处于类似的位置，看起来什么都能做，但同时我们还需要做更多工作，直到真正解决所有问题，让它变得非常好、非常可靠、稳健且行为良好。

turns.en: By 2030, what percent of GDP is AI?
turns.zh: 到 2030 年，AI 占 GDP 的百分比是多少？

turns.en: Oh gosh, hard to answer that question. Very hard to answer that question. Give me an over/under. The problem is that my error bars are in log scale, so I could imagine a huge percentage, I could imagine a disappointingly small percentage at the same time.
turns.zh: 哦天哪，这个问题很难回答。非常难回答。给我一个上下限吧。问题是我的误差范围是对数尺度的，所以我可以想象一个巨大的百分比，也可以同时想象一个令人失望的小百分比。

turns.en: Okay, so let's take the counterfactual where it is a small percentage. Let's say it's 2030 and not that much economic value has been created by these LLMs, as unlikely as you think this might be. What would be your best explanation right now why something like this might happen?
turns.zh: 好的，那我们假设一个反事实情况，即占比很小。假设到了 2030 年，这些大语言模型并没有创造太多经济价值，尽管你认为这不太可能。你现在最好的解释是什么，为什么会出现这种情况？

turns.en: My best explanation—I really don't think that's a likely possibility, so that's the preface to the comment. But if I were to take the premise of your question, why were things disappointing in terms of the real-world impact? My answer would be reliability. If somehow it ends up being the case that you really want them to be reliable and they ended up not being reliable, or if reliability turns out to be harder than we expect—I really don't think that will be the case, but if I had to pick one, it would be reliability. That you still have to look over the answers and double-check everything, and that just really puts a damper on the economic value that can be produced by those systems. They'll be technologically mature; it's just a question of whether they'll be reliable enough.
turns.zh: 我最好的解释——我真的认为这不太可能，所以这是评论的前提。但如果我接受你问题的前提，为什么现实世界的影响令人失望？我的答案是可靠性。如果最终情况是你非常希望它们可靠，但它们却不可靠，或者可靠性比我们预期的更难——我真的认为不会这样，但如果非要选一个，那就是可靠性。你仍然需要检查答案并复核一切，这确实会抑制这些系统所能产生的经济价值。它们在技术上会成熟，只是是否足够可靠的问题。

turns.en: Yeah, in some sense, not reliable means not technologically mature, if you see what I mean.
turns.zh: 是的，在某种意义上，不可靠就意味着技术不成熟，你明白我的意思。

turns.en: Yeah, fair enough.
turns.zh: 是的，有道理。

turns.en: What's after generative models? Right, so before you were working on reinforcement learning. Is this basically it? Is this a paradigm that gets us to AGI, or is there something after this?
turns.zh: 生成式模型之后是什么？之前你在研究强化学习。这基本上就是全部了吗？这是一个能让我们达到 AGI 的范式，还是之后还有别的东西？

turns.en: I mean, I think this paradigm is going to go really, really far, and I would not underestimate it. I think it's quite likely that this exact paradigm is not going to be the key AGI form factor. I hesitate to say precisely what the next paradigm will be, but I think it will probably involve integration of all the different ideas that came in the past.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我认为这个范式会走得非常非常远，我不会低估它。但很有可能这个确切的范式不会成为 AGI 的关键形态。我不太愿意确切地说下一个范式是什么，但我认为它可能会整合过去所有不同的想法。

turns.en: Is there some specific one you're referring to?
turns.zh: 你指的是某个具体的想法吗？

turns.en: I mean, it's hard to be specific. So you could argue that the next-token prediction can only help match human performance, and maybe not surpass it. What would it take to surpass human performance? So I challenge the claim that next-token prediction cannot surpass human performance. It looks...
turns.zh: 我的意思是，很难具体说明。你可以说下一个词预测只能帮助匹配人类表现，也许不能超越。那要怎样才能超越人类表现？所以我质疑下一个词预测无法超越人类表现的说法。它看起来……

turns.en: Like on the surface, if you just learn to imitate and predict what people do, it means you can only copy people. But there is a counterargument for why that might not be quite so.
turns.zh: 表面上，如果你只是学习模仿和预测人们的行为，那就意味着你只能复制别人。但有一个反驳观点认为情况可能并非如此。

turns.en: If your neural net is smart enough, you just ask it: what would a person with great insight and visionary capability do? Maybe such a person doesn't exist, but there's a pretty good chance the neural net will be able to extrapolate how such a person should behave. Do you see what I mean?
turns.zh: 如果你的神经网络足够聪明，你只需问它：一个具有深刻洞察力和远见卓识的人会怎么做？也许这样的人并不存在，但神经网络很有可能会推断出这样的人应该如何行事。你明白我的意思吗？

turns.en: Yes, although where would we get the insight about what that person would do if not from the data of regular people? Because if you think about it, what does it mean to predict the next token well enough? It's a much deeper question than it seems.
turns.zh: 是的，但如果不是从普通人的数据中，我们又如何获得关于那个人会做什么的洞察呢？因为仔细想想，很好地预测下一个词到底意味着什么？这比表面看起来要深刻得多。

turns.en: Predicting the next token well means you understand the underlying reality that led to the creation of that token. It's not just statistics—but what is statistics? To understand the statistics and compress them, you need to understand what about the world creates those statistics. So then you say, okay, I have all these people—what about people creates their behaviors? Well, they have thoughts, feelings, ideas, and they do things in certain ways. All of those would be deduced from next-token prediction. I'd argue that this should make it possible, not indefinitely but to a pretty decent degree, to say: can you guess what you would do if you took a person with this characteristic and that characteristic? Such a person doesn't exist, but because you're so good at predicting the next token, you should still be able to guess what that person would do—this hypothetical imaginary person with far greater mental ability than the rest of us.
turns.zh: 很好地预测下一个词意味着你理解了产生那个词背后的底层现实。这不仅仅是统计学——但统计学又是什么呢？要理解并压缩统计数据，你需要理解世界是如何产生这些统计数据的。所以你会说，好吧，我有这么多人——是什么造就了人们的行为？他们有思想、情感、想法，并以特定方式行事。所有这些都可以从下一个词预测中推导出来。我认为这应该使得——不是无限地，而是在相当程度上——能够说：你能猜出一个具有这样那样特征的人会怎么做吗？这样的人并不存在，但因为你非常擅长预测下一个词，你仍然应该能猜出那个人会做什么——这个假设的、心智能力远超我们其他人的虚构人物。

turns.en: When we're doing reinforcement learning on these models, how long before most of the data for the reinforcement learning is coming from AI and not humans?
turns.zh: 当我们对这些模型进行强化学习时，多久之后强化学习的大部分数据会来自AI而不是人类？

turns.en: Already most of the data for reinforcement learning is coming from AI. Well, it's like the humans are being used to train the reward function, but then the reward function in its interaction with the model is automatic, and all the data generated during the process of reinforcement learning is created by AI. So if you look at the current paradigm—which has been getting significant attention because of ChatGPT—reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), there is human feedback. The human feedback is being used to train the reward function, and then the reward function is being used to create the data which trains the model.
turns.zh: 强化学习的大部分数据已经来自AI了。嗯，人类被用来训练奖励函数，但奖励函数在与模型交互时是自动的，强化学习过程中生成的所有数据都是由AI创造的。所以如果你看看当前的范式——因为ChatGPT而备受关注——基于人类反馈的强化学习（RLHF），有人类反馈。人类反馈被用来训练奖励函数，然后奖励函数被用来创建训练模型的数据。

turns.en: And is there any hope of just removing the human from the loop and having it improve itself in some sort of AlphaGo way?
turns.zh: 有没有希望完全将人类从循环中移除，让它以某种AlphaGo的方式自我改进？

turns.en: Definitely. I mean, I feel like in some sense our hopes—our plan—very much so. The thing you really want is for the human teachers that teach the AI to collaborate with an AI. You might want to think of it as being in a world where the human teachers do 1% of the work and the AI does 99% of the work. You don't want it to be 100% AI, but you do want it to be a human-machine collaboration which teaches the next machine.
turns.zh: 当然。我的意思是，在某种意义上，我们的希望——我们的计划——非常如此。你真正想要的是教导AI的人类教师与AI合作。你可以把它想象成一个世界，人类教师做1%的工作，AI做99%的工作。你不希望它是100%的AI，但你确实希望它是一种人机协作，来教导下一个机器。

turns.en: Currently, I've had a chance to play around with these models. They seem bad at multi-step reasoning, and they are getting better, but what does it take to really surpass that barrier?
turns.zh: 目前，我有机会试用这些模型。它们似乎不擅长多步推理，而且正在变得更好，但要真正突破这个障碍需要什么？

turns.en: I think dedicated training will get us there. More improvements of the base models will get us there. But fundamentally, I also don't feel like they're that bad at multi-step reasoning. I actually think they are bad at mental multi-step reasoning when they're not allowed to think out loud, but when they are allowed to think out loud, they're quite good. And I expect this to improve significantly both with better models and with special training.
turns.zh: 我认为专门的训练会让我们达到目标。基础模型的更多改进也会让我们达到目标。但根本上，我也不觉得它们在多步推理上那么差。我实际上认为，当它们不被允许大声思考时，它们在心理多步推理上很差，但当它们被允许大声思考时，它们相当不错。我预计这会随着更好的模型和专门训练而显著改善。

turns.en: Are we running out of reasoning tokens on the internet? Are there enough of them?
turns.zh: 互联网上的推理词元会用完吗？它们够用吗？

turns.en: For context, there are claims that indeed at some point we will run out of tokens in general to train these models. I think this will happen one day, and by the time that happens, we need to have other ways of training models, other ways of productively improving their capabilities and sharpening their behavior, making sure they're doing exactly what we want without more data. But we haven't run out of data yet. There's more. I would say the data situation is still quite good; there's still lots to go. But at some point, yes, data will run out.
turns.zh: 作为背景，有人声称确实在某个时候我们会用尽训练这些模型所需的词元。我认为这一天终会到来，到那时，我们需要有其他训练模型的方法，有其他方式有效地提升它们的能力并优化它们的行为，确保它们在没有更多数据的情况下完全按照我们的意愿行事。但我们还没有用尽数据。还有更多。我会说数据情况仍然相当不错；还有很多路要走。但到了某个时候，是的，数据会用完。

turns.en: What is the most valuable source of data? Is it Reddit, Twitter, books? What would you trade many other tokens of other varieties for?
turns.zh: 最有价值的数据来源是什么？是Reddit、Twitter、书籍吗？你愿意用许多其他类型的词元来交换什么？

turns.en: Generally speaking, you'd like tokens which are speaking about smarter things, tokens which are more interesting. So all the sources you mentioned are valuable. Okay, so maybe not Twitter.
turns.zh: 一般来说，你会想要谈论更聪明事物的词元，更有趣的词元。所以你提到的所有来源都有价值。好吧，也许不包括Twitter。

turns.en: Do we need to go multimodal to get more tokens, or do we still have enough text tokens left?
turns.zh: 我们需要转向多模态来获取更多词元吗，还是我们仍有足够的文本词元？

turns.en: I think you can still go very far in text only, but going multimodal seems like a very fruitful direction.
turns.zh: 我认为仅靠文本仍然可以走得很远，但转向多模态似乎是一个非常富有成效的方向。

turns.en: If you're comfortable talking about this, where is the place where we haven't scraped the tokens yet?
turns.zh: 如果你方便谈这个，我们还没有抓取词元的地方在哪里？

turns.en: Obviously, I can't answer that question for us, but I'm sure for everyone there's a different answer to that question.
turns.zh: 显然，我不能代表我们回答这个问题，但我相信每个人对此都有不同的答案。

turns.en: How many orders of magnitude improvement can we get just from algorithmic improvement, not from scale or data?
turns.zh: 仅通过算法改进，而不是通过规模或数据，我们能获得多少个数量级的改进？

turns.en: Hard to answer, but I'm sure there is some—a lot or a little. It's only one way to find out.
turns.zh: 很难回答，但我确信有一些——很多或很少。只有一种方法可以找到答案。

turns.en: Let me get your quick-fire opinions about these different research directions. Retrieval-augmented Transformers—just somehow storing the data outside of the model itself and retrieving it—seems promising. Do you see that as a path forward?
turns.zh: 让我听听你对这些不同研究方向的快速看法。检索增强的Transformer——以某种方式将数据存储在模型本身之外并检索——似乎很有前景。你认为这是一条前进的道路吗？

turns.en: It seems promising.
turns.zh: 它看起来很有前景。

turns.en: Robotics—was it the right step for OpenAI to leave that behind?
turns.zh: 机器人技术——OpenAI放弃它是正确的步骤吗？

turns.en: Yes, it was. Back then, it really wasn't possible to continue working in robotics because there was so little data. If you wanted to work on robotics, you needed to become a robotics company—you needed to have a giant group of people working on building robots, maintaining them, and even then, if you're only going to have 100 robots, it's a giant operation already, but you're not going to get that much data. In a world where most of the progress comes from the combination of compute and data—that's where we've been, where it was the combination of compute and data that drove the progress—there was no path to data from robotics. So back in the day, that decision was made to stop working on it.
turns.zh: 是的，那是正确的。当时，继续从事机器人研究几乎不可能，因为数据太少。如果你想研究机器人，你需要成为一家机器人公司——你需要一大群人建造机器人、维护它们，即使如此，如果你只有100个机器人，那已经是巨大的运营了，但你不会得到那么多数据。在一个大部分进步来自算力和数据结合的世界里——我们一直处于这种状态，是算力和数据的结合推动了进步——从机器人技术中获取数据没有路径。所以当时做出了停止研究的决定。

turns.en: In robotics there was no path forward. Is there one now?
turns.zh: 在机器人领域，以前没有前进的道路。现在有了吗？

turns.en: I'd say that now it is possible to create a path forward, but one needs to really commit to the task of robotics. You really need to say, 'I'm going to build many thousands, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of robots and somehow collect data from them and find a gradual path where the robots are doing something slightly more useful, and then the data that is obtained and used to train the models, they do something slightly more useful.' So you could imagine this kind of gradual path of improvement: you build more robots, they do more things, you collect more data, and so on. But you really need to be committed to this path. If you say 'I want to make robotics happen,' that's what you need to do. I believe that there are companies who are thinking about doing exactly that, but I think that you need to really love robots and need to be really willing to solve all the physical and logistical problems of dealing with them. It's not the same as software at all. So I think one could make progress in robotics today with enough motivation.
turns.zh: 我认为现在有可能开辟一条前进的道路，但需要真正致力于机器人技术这个任务。你必须说：‘我要制造成千上万、数十万、甚至上百万台机器人，从它们那里收集数据，并找到一条渐进的道路，让机器人做稍微更有用的事情，然后利用获得的数据训练模型，让它们做稍微更有用的事情。’所以你可以想象这种渐进式的改进路径：你制造更多机器人，它们做更多事情，你收集更多数据，如此循环。但你必须真正致力于这条道路。如果你说‘我想让机器人成为现实’，这就是你需要做的。我相信有些公司正在考虑这样做，但我觉得你必须真正热爱机器人，并且愿意解决所有物理和后勤问题。这和软件完全不同。所以我认为，只要有足够的动力，今天就能在机器人领域取得进展。

turns.en: What ideas are you excited to try but you can't because they don't work well on current hardware?
turns.zh: 有什么想法你很想去尝试，但因为当前硬件运行不佳而无法实现？

turns.en: I don't think current hardware is a limitation. I think it's just not the case.
turns.zh: 我不认为当前硬件是限制。事实并非如此。

turns.en: So anything you want to try you can just spin it up?
turns.zh: 所以任何你想尝试的东西都可以直接启动？

turns.en: I mean, of course, you might say 'I wish current hardware was cheaper' or 'maybe it would be better if it had higher memory or processor bandwidth,' but by and large, hardware is not a limitation.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，当然，你可能会说‘我希望当前硬件更便宜’或者‘如果内存或处理器带宽更高会更好’，但总的来说，硬件不是限制。

turns.en: Let's talk about alignment. Do you think we'll ever have a mathematical definition of alignment?
turns.zh: 我们来谈谈对齐。你认为我们会有对齐的数学定义吗？

turns.en: Mathematical definition, I think, is unlikely. I do think that we will instead have multiple definitions that look at alignment from different aspects, and I think that this is how we will get the assurance that we want. By which I mean, you can look at the behavior in various adversarial stress situations, you can look at how the neural net operates from the inside. I think you have to look at all several of these factors at the same time.
turns.zh: 我认为数学定义不太可能。我确实认为我们会有多个定义，从不同方面审视对齐，我认为这就是我们获得所需保证的方式。我的意思是，你可以观察在各种对抗性压力情境下的行为，你可以从内部观察神经网络如何运作。我认为你必须同时考虑所有这些因素。

turns.en: And how sure do you have to be before you release a model? Is it 100%? 95%?
turns.zh: 在发布模型之前，你需要有多确定？是100%？95%？

turns.en: Well, it depends how capable the model is. The more capable the model is, the more confident you need to be. So if it's something that's almost AGI... well, depends what your AGI can do. Keep in mind, AGI is an ambiguous term. Your average college undergrad is an AGI, right? So depending on where you put this mark, you need to be more or less confident.
turns.zh: 这取决于模型的能力。模型越强大，你需要越自信。所以如果它接近AGI……嗯，取决于你的AGI能做什么。请记住，AGI是一个模糊的术语。普通大学本科生就是AGI，对吧？所以根据你设定的标准，你需要或多或少地自信。

turns.en: You mentioned a few of the paths towards alignment earlier. What is the one you think is most promising at this point?
turns.zh: 你之前提到了几条对齐的路径。你认为目前哪一条最有前景？

turns.en: I think it will be a combination. I really think you will not want to have just one approach. I think people want to have a combination of approaches where we spend a lot of compute adversarially probing to find any mismatch between the behavior that we wanted to teach and the behavior that it exhibits. We look inside into the neural net using another neural net to understand how it operates on the inside. I think all of them will be necessary. Every approach like this reduces the probability of misalignment, and you also want to be in a world where your degree of alignment keeps increasing faster than the capability of the models. I would say that right now our understanding of our models is still quite rudimentary. We made some progress, but much more progress is possible. So I would expect that ultimately the thing that will really succeed is when we will have a small neural net that is well understood that's given the task to study the behavior of a large neural net that is not understood to verify.
turns.zh: 我认为会是多种方法的结合。我真的认为你不会只想用一种方法。我认为人们需要组合多种方法：我们花费大量算力进行对抗性探测，以发现我们想教的行为与模型实际表现之间的任何不匹配。我们使用另一个神经网络从内部观察，以理解它内部如何运作。我认为所有这些方法都是必要的。每种方法都降低了不对齐的概率，而且你还希望处于一个对齐程度增长速度快于模型能力的世界中。我想说，目前我们对模型的理解仍然相当初级。我们取得了一些进展，但还有更多进步空间。所以我预计，最终真正成功的会是：我们有一个被充分理解的小型神经网络，它被赋予任务去研究一个未被理解的大型神经网络的行为，并进行验证。

turns.en: By what point is most of the research being done by AI?
turns.zh: 到什么时候大部分研究将由AI完成？

turns.en: I mean, so today when you use Copilot, right? How do you divide it up? So I expect at some point you ask your descendant of ChatGPT, you say 'Hey, I'm thinking about this and this, can you suggest fruitful ideas I should try?' and you would actually get fruitful ideas. And I think that will make it possible for you to solve problems you couldn't solve before.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，今天当你使用Copilot时，对吧？你怎么分配？所以我预计在某个时候，你会问ChatGPT的后代：‘嘿，我在考虑这个和那个，你能建议一些我应该尝试的有成果的想法吗？’然后你确实会得到有成果的想法。我认为这将使你能够解决以前无法解决的问题。

turns.en: But it's somehow just telling humans giving them ideas faster, it's not itself interacting with the world?
turns.zh: 但这只是更快地给人类提供想法，它本身并没有与世界互动？

turns.en: I mean, you could slice it in a variety of ways, but I think the bottleneck there is good ideas, good insights, and that's something which the neural net could help with.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你可以从不同角度切入，但我认为瓶颈在于好的想法和洞见，而这正是神经网络可以提供帮助的。

turns.en: If you could design a billion-dollar prize for some sort of alignment research result or product, what is the concrete criterion set for that billion-dollar prize? Something that makes sense for such a prize.
turns.zh: 如果你能设计一个十亿美元的奖项，奖励某种对齐研究结果或产品，那么具体标准是什么？什么样的标准对这个奖项有意义？

turns.en: It's funny you asked this. I was actually thinking about this exact question. I haven't come up with an exact criteria yet. Maybe something like a prize where we could say that two years later, or three years later, or five years later, we'll look back and say 'that was the main result.' So rather than say that there is a prize committee that decides right away, we wait for five years and then award it retroactively. But there's no concrete thing we can identify yet, like 'you solve this particular problem and you made a lot of progress.' I think a lot of progress, yes, I wouldn't say that this would be the full thing.
turns.zh: 你问这个很有趣。我实际上也在思考这个问题。我还没有想出确切的标准。也许可以设立一个奖项，让我们在两年、三年或五年后回顾时可以说‘那是主要成果’。所以不是有一个评审委员会立即决定，而是等五年后追溯颁发。但目前还没有具体的东西可以确定，比如‘你解决了这个特定问题，取得了很大进展’。我认为是很大进展，但不会说这就是全部。

turns.en: Do you think end-to-end training is the right architecture for bigger and bigger models, or do we need better ways of just connecting things together?
turns.zh: 你认为端到端训练是越来越大模型的正确架构，还是我们需要更好的方式来连接事物？

turns.en: I think end-to-end training is very promising. I think connecting things together is very promising. Everything is promising.
turns.zh: 我认为端到端训练非常有前景。我认为连接事物也非常有前景。一切都有前景。

turns.en: OpenAI is projecting revenues of a billion dollars in 2024. That might very well be correct, but I'm just curious: when you're talking about a new general-purpose technology, how do you estimate how big a windfall it will be? Why that particular number?
turns.zh: OpenAI预计2024年收入将达到10亿美元。这很可能正确，但我很好奇：当谈论一种新的通用技术时，你如何估计它会有多大的收益？为什么是那个具体数字？

turns.en: I mean, you look at the current data. We've already had a product for quite a while now, from the GPT-3 days from two years ago through the API, and we've seen how it grew. We've seen how the response to DALL-E has grown as well, and how the response to ChatGPT has grown. I think all of this gives us information that allows us to make a relatively sensible extrapolation to 2024. Maybe that would be one answer: you need to have data. You can't come up with those things out of thin air, because otherwise your error bars will be off by 100x in each direction.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你看当前的数据。我们从两年前的GPT-3时代开始，通过API已经有了相当长时间的产品，我们看到了它的增长。我们也看到了DALL-E和ChatGPT的响应增长。我认为所有这些都为我们提供了信息，使我们能够对2024年做出相对合理的推断。也许这就是一个答案：你需要有数据。你不能凭空想出这些数字，否则你的误差范围在每个方向都会偏差100倍。

turns.en: Exponentials don't stay exponential, especially when they get into bigger and bigger quantities, right? So how do you determine in this case that... I mean, would you bet against them? Not after talking with you. Let's talk about what a post-AI future looks like. So are people like you—I'm guessing you're working like 80-hour weeks towards some grand goal that you're really obsessed with—are you going to be satisfied in a world where you're basically living in an AI retirement home? Or what is your concrete plan after AI comes?
turns.zh: 指数增长不会一直持续，尤其是当规模越来越大时，对吧？那在这种情况下你怎么判断……我是说，你会押注它们会放缓吗？跟你聊过之后不会了。我们来谈谈后AI时代的样子吧。像你这样的人——我猜你每周工作80小时，朝着某个让你痴迷的伟大目标努力——在一个基本上就像AI养老院的世界里，你会满足吗？或者AI到来后你具体打算做什么？

turns.en: I think the question of what I'll be doing or what people will be doing after AI comes is a very tricky question. Where will people find meaning? But I think that's something AI could help with. One thing I imagine is that we'll all be able to become more enlightened because we'll interact with an AGI that will help us see the world more correctly, become better on the inside as a result of interaction. Imagine talking to the best meditation teacher in history. I think that will be helpful. But I also think that because the world will change a lot, it will be very hard for people to understand what is happening precisely and how to really contribute. One thing that some people will choose to do is to become part AI in order to expand their minds and understanding, to really be able to solve the hardest problems that society will face.
turns.zh: 我认为AI到来后我会做什么，或者人们会做什么，这是一个非常棘手的问题。人们从哪里找到意义？但我认为这正是AI可以帮助我们的地方。我想象的一件事是，我们都能变得更加开悟，因为我们会与一个AGI互动，它帮助我们更正确地看待世界，从而在内心变得更好。想象一下与历史上最好的冥想老师交谈。我认为那会很有帮助。但我也认为，因为世界会发生巨大变化，人们很难准确理解正在发生什么以及如何真正做出贡献。有些人会选择成为半AI，以扩展他们的心智和理解力，从而真正解决社会将面临的最棘手问题。

turns.en: Are you going to become part AI?
turns.zh: 你会成为半AI吗？

turns.en: Very tempting. It is tempting, yeah.
turns.zh: 非常诱人。确实很诱人，是的。

turns.en: What do you think? Will there be physically embodied humans in 3000? How do I know what's going to happen in 3000? What does it look like? Are there still humans walking around on Earth? Or have you guys thought concretely about what you actually want this world to look like in 3000?
turns.zh: 你怎么看？公元3000年还会有实体人类吗？我怎么知道3000年会发生什么？那会是什么样子？地球上还有人类在走动吗？或者你们有没有具体想过，你们到底希望这个世界在3000年变成什么样？

turns.en: Well, let me describe what I think is not quite right about the question. It implies that we get to decide how we want the world to look like. I don't think that picture is correct. I think change is the only constant. So of course, even after AGI is built, it doesn't mean the world will be static. The world will continue to change, evolve, and go through all kinds of transformations. I really have no idea—I don't think anyone has any idea—how the world will look in 3000. But I do hope that there will be a lot of descendants of human beings who will live happy, fulfilled lives, free to do as they wish, solving their own problems. One thing I would not want—one world I would find very unexciting—is one where we build this powerful tool and then the government says, 'Okay, the AGI said society should be run in such a way, so now we should run society that way.' I'd much rather have a world where people are still free to make their own mistakes, suffer the consequences, and gradually evolve morally and progress forward on their own through their own strength, with the AGI providing more like a base safety net.
turns.zh: 嗯，让我描述一下我认为这个问题不太对的地方。它暗示我们可以决定我们希望世界变成什么样。我不认为这种看法是正确的。我认为变化是唯一不变的。所以当然，即使在AGI建成之后，世界也不会静止。世界会继续变化、演化，经历各种转型。我真的不知道——我认为没人知道——3000年的世界会是什么样。但我确实希望会有很多人类的后代，过着幸福、充实的生活，自由地做自己想做的事，解决自己的问题。有一件事我不希望看到——一个我觉得非常无趣的世界——就是我们建造了这个强大的工具，然后政府说：‘好吧，AGI说社会应该这样运行，所以我们现在就按这样运行。’我更喜欢一个世界，人们仍然可以自由地犯自己的错误，承受后果，并逐渐在道德上进化，通过自己的力量向前进步，而AGI更像是提供一个基本的安全网。

turns.en: How much time do you spend thinking about these kinds of things versus just doing the research?
turns.zh: 你花多少时间思考这类事情，而不是只做研究？

turns.en: I do think about those things a fair bit. Yeah, I think those are very interesting questions.
turns.zh: 我确实会花相当多的时间思考这些。是的，我认为这些都是非常有趣的问题。

turns.en: In what ways have the capabilities we have today surpassed where you expected them to be in 2015? And in what ways are they still not where you expected them to be by this point?
turns.zh: 我们今天拥有的能力在哪些方面超出了你在2015年的预期？又在哪些方面还没有达到你当时的预期？

turns.en: In 2015, my thinking was a lot more—I just didn't want to bet against deep learning. I wanted to make the biggest possible bet on deep learning. I didn't know how, but it would figure it out.
turns.zh: 2015年，我的想法更多是——我只是不想押注深度学习会失败。我想对深度学习做出尽可能大的赌注。我不知道具体怎么做，但它会自己找到办法。

turns.en: Is there any specific way in which it's been more than you expected or less than you expected? Some concrete prediction you had in 2015 that's been announced?
turns.zh: 有没有哪个具体方面超出了你的预期或低于你的预期？你2015年做过的一些具体预测后来实现了？

turns.en: Unfortunately, I don't remember concrete predictions I made in 2015. But I definitely think that overall in 2015, I just wanted to make the biggest bet possible on deep learning. I didn't have a specific idea of how far things would go in seven years. In 2015, I did have these bets with people in 2016, maybe 2017, that things would go really far. But specifics? It's both the case that it surprised me and I was making these aggressive predictions, but I think maybe I believed them only 50% on the inside.
turns.zh: 不幸的是，我不记得2015年做过什么具体的预测。但我确实认为，总的来说在2015年，我只是想对深度学习做出最大的赌注。我没有具体想法七年内事情会发展到什么程度。2015年，我确实和一些人打赌，在2016年或2017年，事情会走得很远。但具体细节？它既让我惊讶，我也在做出这些激进的预测，但我想也许我内心只有50%相信它们。

turns.en: What do you believe now that even most people at OpenAI would find farfetched?
turns.zh: 你现在相信什么，甚至OpenAI的大多数人都会觉得牵强？

turns.en: I think that because we communicate a lot, OpenAI people have a pretty good sense of what I think. So we've reached a point at OpenAI where I think we see eye to eye on all these questions.
turns.zh: 我认为因为我们交流很多，OpenAI的人对我的想法有很好的了解。所以在OpenAI我们已经到了一个地步，我觉得我们在所有这些问题上都看法一致。

turns.en: Google has its custom TPU hardware, all this data from all its users, Gmail, and so on. Does it give it an advantage in terms of training bigger and better models than you?
turns.zh: 谷歌有它定制的TPU硬件，还有来自所有用户的数据，比如Gmail等等。这是否让它在训练更大更好的模型方面比你们有优势？

turns.en: I think when the TPU first came out, I was really impressed and thought, 'Wow, this is amazing.' But that's because I didn't quite understand hardware back then. What really turned out to be the case is that TPUs and GPUs are almost the same thing. They are very, very similar. A GPU chip is a little bit bigger, a TPU chip is a little bit smaller, maybe a bit cheaper. But then they make more GPUs than TPUs, so I think GPUs might be cheaper after all. But fundamentally, you have a big processor and a lot of memory, and there is a bottleneck link between those two. The problem both the TPU and GPU are trying to solve is that by the time it takes to move one floating point from memory to the processor, you can do several hundred floating point operations on the processor. That means you have to do some kind of batch processing. In this sense, both architectures are the same. I really feel like hardware—in some sense, the only thing that matters about hardware is cost per flop, overall system cost. There isn't that much difference. Actually, I don't know what the TPU costs are, but I would suspect that if anything, GPUs are more expensive because there are fewer of them.
turns.zh: 我认为当TPU刚出来时，我印象非常深刻，心想：‘哇，这太棒了。’但那是因为我当时对硬件不太了解。实际情况是，TPU和GPU几乎是同一回事。它们非常非常相似。GPU芯片稍微大一点，TPU芯片稍微小一点，可能便宜一点。但随后他们生产的GPU比TPU多，所以我认为GPU可能反而更便宜。但根本上，你有一个大处理器和大量内存，两者之间存在瓶颈链接。TPU和GPU都在试图解决的问题是：将一个浮点数从内存移动到处理器所需的时间内，你可以在处理器上执行几百次浮点运算。这意味着你必须进行某种批处理。在这个意义上，两种架构是一样的。我真的觉得硬件——从某种意义上说，硬件唯一重要的是每浮点运算的成本，整体系统成本。没有太大区别。实际上，我不知道TPU的成本是多少，但我怀疑如果有什么不同的话，GPU更贵，因为它们的数量更少。

turns.en: When you're doing your work, how much of the time is spent configuring the right initializations, making sure the training run goes well, and getting the right hyperparameters? And how much is it just coming up with whole new ideas?
turns.zh: 当你做研究时，有多少时间花在配置正确的初始化、确保训练运行顺利以及找到合适的超参数上？又有多少时间只是提出全新的想法？

turns.en: I would say it's a combination. But coming up with whole new ideas is actually a modest part of the work. Certainly coming up with...
turns.zh: 我会说两者结合。但提出全新的想法实际上只是工作中很小的一部分。当然，提出……

turns.en: New ideas are important, but I think even more important is to understand the results, to understand the existing ideas, to understand what's going on. Because normally you have this very complicated system, you run it, and you get some behavior which is hard to understand. Understanding the results, figuring out what next experiment to run—a lot of the time is spent on that. Understanding what could be wrong, what could have caused the neural net to produce a result which was not expected. I'd say a lot of time we spend as well, of course, coming up with new ideas. But not new ideas—I don't like this framing as much. It's not that it's false, but I think the main activity is actually understanding.
turns.zh: 新想法固然重要，但我认为更重要的是理解结果、理解现有想法、理解正在发生的事情。因为通常你有一个非常复杂的系统，运行它，然后得到一些难以理解的行为。理解结果、弄清楚下一步该做什么实验——大部分时间都花在这上面。理解可能哪里出错了，是什么导致神经网络产生了意想不到的结果。当然，我们也会花很多时间想新点子。但我不太喜欢这种说法——不是说它不对，而是我觉得主要活动其实是理解。

turns.en: What do you see as the difference between the two?
turns.zh: 你觉得这两者有什么区别？

turns.en: At least in my mind, when you say 'come up with new ideas,' I think 'oh, what if it did such and such?' Whereas understanding is more like 'what is this whole thing? What are the real underlying phenomena? What are the underlying effects? Why are we doing things this way and not another way?' Of course, this is very adjacent to what can be described as coming up with ideas, but I think the understanding part is where the real action takes place.
turns.zh: 至少在我看来，当你说‘想出新点子’时，我会想‘哦，如果它做了这样那样会怎样？’而理解更像是‘这整个东西是什么？真正的底层现象是什么？底层效应是什么？为什么我们这样做而不是那样做？’当然，这跟‘想出新点子’很接近，但我认为真正的行动在于理解。

turns.en: Does that describe your entire career? Like if you think back on ImageNet or something, was that more a new idea or more understanding?
turns.zh: 这能描述你的整个职业生涯吗？比如回想一下 ImageNet 之类的东西，那更多是新想法还是理解？

turns.en: Oh, it was definitely understanding. Definitely understanding. It was a new understanding of very old things.
turns.zh: 哦，那绝对是理解。绝对是理解。那是对非常古老事物的新理解。

turns.en: What has the experience of training on Azure been like?
turns.zh: 在 Azure 上训练的感受如何？

turns.en: Using Azure? Fantastic. I mean, Microsoft has been a very, very good partner for us, and they've really helped take Azure and bring it to a point where it's really good for ML. We're super happy with it.
turns.zh: 用 Azure？太棒了。我是说，微软一直是我们非常好的合作伙伴，他们真的帮助 Azure 达到了非常适合机器学习的水平。我们非常满意。

ts.sec: Vulnerability of AI Ecosystem to Taiwan Events

turns.en: How vulnerable is the whole AI ecosystem to something that might happen in Taiwan? So let's say there's a tsunami in Taiwan or something. What would happen to AI in general?
turns.zh: 整个 AI 生态系统对台湾可能发生的事情有多脆弱？比如说台湾发生了海啸之类的。AI 整体会怎样？

turns.en: It's definitely going to be a significant setback. It might be something equivalent to no one being able to get more compute for a few years. But I expect compute will spring up. For example, I believe that Intel has fabs from a few generations ago. So if Intel wanted to, they could produce something GPU-like from four years ago. So yeah, it's not the best. I'm actually not sure if my statement about Intel is correct, but I do know that there are fabs outside of Taiwan. They're just not as good, but you can still use them and still go very far with them. It's just a setback.
turns.zh: 这绝对是一个重大的挫折。可能相当于几年内没人能获得更多算力。但我预计算力会涌现出来。例如，我相信英特尔有几代前的工厂。所以如果英特尔愿意，他们可以生产四年前类似 GPU 的东西。所以，这不是最好的情况。我其实不确定关于英特尔的说法是否正确，但我知道台湾以外也有工厂。它们只是没那么好，但你仍然可以用它们，并且仍然可以走得很远。这只是一个挫折。

turns.en: Won't inference become cost prohibitive as these models get bigger and bigger?
turns.zh: 随着这些模型越来越大，推理不会变得成本过高吗？

turns.en: I have a different way of looking at this question. It's not that inference will become cost prohibitive. Inference of better models will indeed become more expensive, but is it prohibitive? Well, it depends on how useful it is. If it is more useful, then even if it is expensive, it is not prohibitive. To give you an analogy: suppose you want to talk to a lawyer. You have some case or need some advice. You are perfectly happy to spend $500 an hour, right? So if your neural net could give you really reliable legal advice, you'd say 'I'm happy to spend $400 for that advice.' And suddenly inference becomes very much non-prohibitive. The question is: can a neural net produce an answer good enough at this cost? Yes. And you'll just have price discrimination—different models for different use cases. It's already the case today. On our product, the API, we serve multiple neural nets of different sizes, and different customers use different neural nets depending on their use case. If someone can take a small model and fine-tune it and get something satisfactory, they'll use that. But if someone wants to do something more complicated and interesting, they'll use the biggest model.
turns.zh: 我对这个问题有不同的看法。并不是说推理会变得成本过高。更好的模型的推理确实会更贵，但算不算过高？这取决于它有多有用。如果它更有用，那么即使贵，也不算过高。打个比方：假设你想和律师谈谈。你有个案子或需要一些建议。你完全愿意每小时花 500 美元，对吧？所以如果你的神经网络能给你非常可靠的法律建议，你会说‘我愿意花 400 美元买这个建议。’突然之间，推理就变得非常不昂贵了。问题是：神经网络能否以这个成本产生足够好的答案？能。而且你会有价格歧视——不同模型用于不同用例。今天已经是这样了。在我们的产品 API 中，我们提供多种不同大小的神经网络，不同客户根据他们的用例使用不同大小的神经网络。如果有人能用一个小模型微调并得到满意的结果，他们就会用那个。但如果有人想做更复杂、更有趣的事情，他们会用最大的模型。

turns.en: How do you prevent these models from just becoming commodities, where different companies just undercut each other's prices until it's basically the cost of the GPU run?
turns.zh: 你如何防止这些模型变成商品，各家公司互相压价，直到基本上就是 GPU 运行的成本？

turns.en: I think there is without question a force that's trying to create that, and the answer is you've got to keep on making progress. You've got to keep improving the models, keep coming up with new ideas, and making our models better, more reliable, more trustworthy, so you can trust their answers—all those things.
turns.zh: 我认为毫无疑问有一股力量在试图制造这种局面，答案是你要不断取得进展。你必须不断改进模型，不断提出新想法，让我们的模型更好、更可靠、更值得信赖，这样你才能相信它们的答案——所有这些。

turns.en: But let's say it's 2025 and the model from 2024 is being offered at cost, and it's still pretty good. Why would people use a new one from 2025 if the one from just a year older is even better?
turns.zh: 但假设现在是 2025 年，2024 年的模型以成本价提供，而且仍然很好。如果只老一年的模型甚至更好，为什么人们会用 2025 年的新模型？

turns.en: There are several answers there. For some use cases, that may be true. There will be a new model from 2025 which will be driving the more interesting use cases. There's also going to be a question of inference cost—you can do research to serve the same model at less cost. So the same model will be served at different costs by different companies. I can also imagine some degree of specialization, where some companies may try to specialize in some area and be stronger in that area compared to other companies. I think that may be a response to commoditization to some degree.
turns.zh: 有几个答案。对于某些用例，这可能是真的。2025 年会有新模型驱动更有趣的用例。还有一个推理成本的问题——你可以通过研究以更低的成本提供相同的模型。所以同一模型由不同公司提供时成本会不同。我还可以想象一定程度的专业化，一些公司可能试图在某个领域专业化，并比其他公司更强。我认为这在一定程度上可能是对商品化的回应。

ts.sec: Convergence and Divergence in Research Directions

turns.en: Over time, do these different companies' research directions converge or diverge? Are they doing similar things over time, or are they branching off into different areas?
turns.zh: 随着时间的推移，这些不同公司的研究方向是趋同还是分化？它们是在做类似的事情，还是分支到不同的领域？

turns.en: I'd say in the near term, it looks like there is convergence. I expect this to be a convergence-divergence-convergence behavior. There is a lot of convergence on the near-term work. There's going to be some divergence on the longer term, but once the longer-term work starts to bear fruit, I think there will be convergence again.
turns.zh: 我会说在短期内，看起来是趋同的。我预计这会是趋同-分化-趋同的行为。短期工作有很多趋同。长期会有一些分化，但一旦长期工作开始产生成果，我认为又会趋同。

turns.en: Got it. When one of them finds the most promising area, everybody just goes there?
turns.zh: 明白了。当其中一个找到了最有前景的领域，大家就都涌向那里？

turns.en: That's right. Now there is obviously less publishing now, so it will take longer before this promising direction gets rediscovered, but that's how I imagine it. I think it's going to be convergence, divergence, convergence.
turns.zh: 没错。现在显然发表得少了，所以这个有前景的方向被重新发现需要更长时间，但我是这么想象的。我认为会是趋同、分化、再趋同。

ts.sec: Security Concerns: Spies and Weight Leakage

turns.en: As foreign governments learn about how capable these models are, are you worried about spies or some sort of attack to get your weights, or somehow abuse these models?
turns.zh: 随着外国政府了解到这些模型的能力，你是否担心间谍或某种攻击来获取你的权重，或者以某种方式滥用这些模型？

turns.en: Yeah, it's definitely something that you absolutely can't discount. It's something that we guard against to the best of our ability, but it's going to be a problem for everyone who's building this.
turns.zh: 是的，这绝对是绝对不能忽视的事情。我们尽最大努力防范，但这将是每个构建这些东西的人都会面临的问题。

turns.en: How do you prevent your weights from leaking? I mean, you have really good security people. How many people?
turns.zh: 你们如何防止权重泄露？我是说，你们有非常好的安全人员。有多少人？

turns.en: If they wanted to just stage into the weights, how many people could do that?
turns.zh: 如果他们想直接潜入权重，有多少人能做到？

turns.en: What I can say is that the security people have done a really good job, so that I'm really not worried about the weights being leaked.
turns.zh: 我能说的是，安全团队做得非常好，所以我真的不担心权重泄露。

turns.en: What kinds of emerging properties are you expecting from these models at this scale? Is there something that just comes about de novo?
turns.zh: 你期望这些模型在如此规模下会出现什么样的涌现特性？会有全新的东西出现吗？

turns.en: I'm sure things will come. I'm sure really new surprising properties will come up. I would not be surprised. The thing I'm really excited about, or the thing I'd like to see, is reliability and controllability. I think that will be a very important class of emergent properties. If you have reliability and controllability, I think that helps you solve a lot of problems. Reliability means you can trust the model's output. Controllability means you can control it. We'll see, but it'll be very cool if those emergent properties did exist.
turns.zh: 我相信会有新东西出现。我相信真正令人惊讶的新特性会出现。我不会感到惊讶。我真正兴奋或想看到的是可靠性和可控性。我认为这将是一类非常重要的涌现特性。如果你有可靠性和可控性，那能帮你解决很多问题。可靠性意味着你可以信任模型的输出。可控性意味着你可以控制它。我们拭目以待，但如果这些涌现特性真的存在，那就太酷了。

turns.en: Is there some way you can predict it in advance, like what will happen at this parameter count, what will happen at that?
turns.zh: 有没有办法提前预测，比如在这个参数量下会发生什么，在那个参数量下会发生什么？

turns.en: I think it's possible to make some predictions about specific capabilities, though it's definitely not simple and you can't do it in a super fine-grained way, at least today. But I think getting better at that is really important. Anyone who is interested and has research ideas on how to do that, I think that can be a valuable contribution.
turns.zh: 我认为对特定能力做出一些预测是可能的，尽管这绝对不简单，而且至少在今天，你无法以非常精细的方式做到。但我认为在这方面做得更好非常重要。任何有兴趣并有研究想法的人，我认为那可以是一个有价值的贡献。

turns.en: How seriously do you take the scaling laws? If there's a paper that says you need this many orders of magnitude more to get all the reasoning out, do you take that seriously, or do you think it breaks down at some point?
turns.zh: 你有多认真对待缩放定律？如果有论文说你需要这么多数量级才能得到所有推理能力，你认真对待吗，还是认为它在某个点会失效？

turns.en: The scaling law tells you what happens to your next-token prediction accuracy. There is a whole separate challenge of linking next-token prediction accuracy to reasoning capability. I do believe that indeed there is a link, but this link is complicated. We may find that there are other things that can give us more reasoning per unit effort, like for example some special reasoning tokens. I think they can be helpful.
turns.zh: 缩放定律告诉你的是下一个词预测准确率的变化。将下一个词预测准确率与推理能力联系起来是一个完全独立的挑战。我确实相信存在联系，但这种联系很复杂。我们可能会发现其他东西能让我们在单位努力下获得更多推理能力，比如一些特殊的推理词元。我认为它们会有帮助。

turns.en: Is this something you're considering, just hiring humans to generate tokens for you, or is it all going to come from stuff that already exists out there?
turns.zh: 这是你正在考虑的事情吗，只是雇佣人类为你生成词元，还是全部来自已经存在的东西？

turns.en: I think that relying on people to teach our models to do things, especially to make sure that they are well-behaved and they don't produce false things, is an extremely sensible thing to do.
turns.zh: 我认为依靠人类来教我们的模型做事，特别是确保它们行为良好且不产生虚假内容，是非常明智的做法。

turns.en: Isn't it odd that we have the data we need at exactly the same time as we have the Transformer, at the exact same time that we have these GPUs? Is it odd to you that all these things happen at the same time, or do you not see it that way?
turns.zh: 我们恰好同时拥有所需的数据、Transformer 和这些 GPU，这难道不奇怪吗？你觉得所有这些事情同时发生很奇怪，还是你不这么看？

turns.en: It is definitely an interesting situation. I will say that it is odd, and it is less odd on some level. Here is why it's less odd. The driving force behind the fact that the data exists, that the GPUs exist, that the Transformer exists, is that computers became better and cheaper. We've got smaller and smaller transistors, and suddenly at some point it became economical for every person to have a personal computer. Once everyone has a personal computer, you really want to connect to the network, you get the internet. Once you have the internet, you have suddenly data appearing in great quantities. The GPUs were improving concurrently because you have smaller and smaller transistors and you're looking for things to do with them. Gaming turned out to be a thing that you could do, and then at some point Nvidia said, 'Wait a second, let's turn it into a general-purpose GPU. Maybe someone will find it useful.' Turns out it's good for neural nets. So it could have been the case that maybe the GPU would have arrived 5 years later or 10 years later if gaming wasn't a thing. It's kind of hard to imagine what it means if gaming isn't a thing, but there could be a counterfactual world where GPUs arrived 5 years after the data or 5 years before the data. In that case, maybe things would have been as ready to go as they are now. But that's the picture: all this progress in all these dimensions is very intertwined. It's not a coincidence. You don't get to pick and choose which dimensions things improve in, if you see what I mean.
turns.zh: 这绝对是一个有趣的情况。我会说这很奇怪，但在某种程度上又不那么奇怪。原因如下：数据存在、GPU 存在、Transformer 存在背后的驱动力是计算机变得更好更便宜。晶体管越来越小，突然在某个时刻，每个人拥有一台个人电脑变得经济可行。一旦每个人都有个人电脑，你就想连接到网络，于是有了互联网。一旦有了互联网，突然就出现了大量数据。GPU 也在同时改进，因为晶体管越来越小，你需要找事情做。游戏成了你可以做的事情，然后某个时刻英伟达说：‘等等，我们把它变成通用 GPU，也许有人会觉得有用。’结果发现它对神经网络很好。所以，如果游戏不存在，GPU 可能会晚 5 年或 10 年出现。很难想象游戏不存在意味着什么，但可能存在一个反事实的世界，GPU 比数据晚 5 年或早 5 年出现。那样的话，事情可能就不会像现在这样准备就绪。但这就是图景：所有这些维度的进步都是紧密交织的。这不是巧合。你不能选择哪些维度进步，你明白我的意思吗？

turns.en: How inevitable is this kind of progress? If you and Geoffrey Hinton and a few other pioneers were never born, does the deep learning revolution happen around the same time? How much does it delay?
turns.zh: 这种进步有多不可避免？如果你和杰弗里·辛顿以及其他几位先驱从未出生，深度学习革命会在差不多同一时间发生吗？会延迟多久？

turns.en: I think maybe there would have been some delay, maybe like a year delay. It's really hard to tell. I hesitate to give a longer answer because, okay, then you'd have GPUs keep on improving, right? Then at some point, I cannot see how someone would not have discovered it. Here's the other thing: if no one has done it, computers keep getting faster and better, it becomes easier and easier to train these neural nets because you have bigger GPUs, so it takes less engineering effort to train one. You don't need to optimize your code as much. When the ImageNet dataset came out, it was huge and very difficult to use. Now imagine you wait for a few years and it becomes very easy to download, and people can just tinker. So I would imagine a modest number of years maximum. This would be my guess. I hesitate to give a longer answer though. You can't rerun the world; you don't know.
turns.zh: 我认为可能会有一些延迟，也许一年左右。很难说。我不太想给出更长的回答，因为，好吧，GPU 会继续改进，对吧？然后在某个时刻，我看不出怎么会有人不发现它。还有一点：如果没人做过，计算机会越来越快越来越好，训练这些神经网络会越来越容易，因为你有更大的 GPU，所以训练一个所需的工程努力更少。你不需要过多优化代码。当 ImageNet 数据集出来时，它很大且非常难用。现在想象你等几年，它变得非常容易下载，人们可以随便摆弄。所以我猜测最多几年。这是我的猜测。不过我不太想给出更长的回答。你不能重来世界；你不知道。

turns.en: Let's go back to alignment for a second. As somebody who deeply understands these models, what is your intuition of how hard alignment will be?
turns.zh: 让我们回到对齐问题。作为一个深刻理解这些模型的人，你直觉上觉得对齐会有多难？

turns.en: I think with the current level of capabilities, we have a pretty good set of ideas of how to align them. But I would not underestimate the difficulty of alignment of models that are actually smarter than us, of models that are capable of misrepresenting their intentions. I think it's something to think about a lot and to research. I think this is one area where academic researchers can make very meaningful contributions.
turns.zh: 我认为在当前的能力水平下，我们有一整套很好的想法来对齐它们。但我不会低估对齐比我们更聪明的模型的难度，那些能够歪曲自己意图的模型。我认为这是一个需要深入思考和研究的领域。我认为这是学术研究者可以做出非常有意义贡献的领域之一。

turns.en: Do you think academia will come up with important insights about actual capabilities, or is that going to be just the companies at this point?
turns.zh: 你认为学术界会提出关于实际能力的重要见解，还是说在这一点上只会是公司？

turns.en: The companies will realize the capabilities. I think it's very possible for academic research to come up with those insights. I think so.
turns.zh: 公司会实现这些能力。我认为学术研究非常有可能提出这些见解。我是这么认为的。

turns.en: It's just it doesn't seem to happen that much for some reason. But I don't think there's anything fundamental about academia. Like it's not like academia can't. I think maybe they're just not thinking about the right problems or something, because maybe it's just easier to see what needs to be done inside these companies.
turns.zh: 只是出于某种原因，这种情况似乎不太常见。但我不认为学术界有什么根本性的问题。并不是说学术界做不到。我想可能他们只是没有思考正确的问题，因为在这些公司内部更容易看清需要做什么。

turns.en: I see. But there's a possibility that somebody could just realize, 'Yeah, I totally... why would I possibly rule this out?' You see what I mean?
turns.zh: 我明白。但有可能有人会突然意识到，‘是的，我完全……我为什么要排除这种可能性呢？’你明白我的意思吗？

turns.en: What are the concrete steps by which these language models start actually impacting the world of atoms, and not just the world of bits?
turns.zh: 这些语言模型通过哪些具体步骤开始真正影响原子世界，而不仅仅是比特世界？

turns.en: Well, you see, I don't think that there is a clean distinction between the world of bits and the world of atoms. Suppose the neural net tells you that, 'Hey, here is something that you should do, and it's going to improve your life, but you need to rearrange your apartment in a certain way.' Then you go and you rearrange your apartment as a result. Did the neural net impact the world of atoms?
turns.zh: 嗯，你看，我不认为比特世界和原子世界之间有明确的界限。假设神经网络告诉你，‘嘿，这里有一些你应该做的事情，它会改善你的生活，但你需要以某种方式重新布置你的公寓。’然后你因此去重新布置了你的公寓。神经网络是否影响了原子世界？

turns.en: Yeah, fair enough. Fair enough.
turns.zh: 是的，有道理。有道理。

turns.en: Do you think it'll take a couple of additional breakthroughs as important as the Transformer to get to superintelligence, or do you think we basically got the insights in the books somewhere and we just need to implement them and connect them?
turns.zh: 你认为要达到超级智能，还需要几个像 Transformer 一样重要的突破，还是说我们基本上已经在某本书中获得了洞察，只需要实现并连接它们？

turns.en: So I don't really see such a big distinction between those two cases, and let me explain why. I think one of the ways in which progress has taken place in the past is that we've understood that something had a desirable property all along, but you didn't realize. So is that a breakthrough? You can say yes it is. Is it an implementation of something on the books? Also yes. So I feel that a few of those are quite likely to happen, but that in hindsight it will not feel like a breakthrough. Everybody is going to say, 'Oh well, of course, it's totally obvious that such and such thing can work.' You see, with the Transformer, the reason it's being brought up as a big specific advance is because it's the kind of thing that was not obvious for almost anyone. So people can say, 'Yeah, it's not something which they knew about.' But if an advance comes from something like, let's consider the most fundamental advance of deep learning: that a big neural network trained with backpropagation can do a lot of things. Where's the novelty? It's not in the neural network, it's not in the backpropagation, but then somehow it was most definitely a giant conceptual breakthrough because for the longest time people just didn't see that. But now everyone sees it, everyone is going to say, 'Well of course, it's totally obvious, big neural networks, everyone knows that they can do it.'
turns.zh: 所以我不太认为这两种情况之间有太大的区别，让我解释一下原因。我认为过去取得进展的方式之一是，我们理解到某物一直具有某种理想属性，但你没有意识到。那么这是一个突破吗？你可以说是的。它是书本上某物的实现吗？也是的。所以我觉得其中一些很可能会发生，但事后看来，它不会感觉像是一个突破。每个人都会说，‘哦，当然，某某东西能工作是完全显而易见的。’你看，对于 Transformer，它被提出来作为一个重大的具体进展，是因为它几乎是任何人都没有意识到的东西。所以人们可以说，‘是的，这不是他们知道的事情。’但如果一个进展来自于像——让我们考虑深度学习最根本的进展：一个用反向传播训练的大型神经网络可以做很多事情。新颖性在哪里？不在神经网络，也不在反向传播，但不知何故，它绝对是一个巨大的概念性突破，因为在很长一段时间里，人们就是没有看到这一点。但现在每个人都看到了，每个人都会说，‘当然，这完全显而易见，大型神经网络，每个人都知道它们能做到。’

turns.en: What is your opinion of your former advisor's new forward-forward algorithm?
turns.zh: 你对你前导师的新前向-前向算法有什么看法？

turns.en: I think that it's an attempt to train a neural network without backpropagation, and I think that this is especially interesting if you are motivated to try to understand how the brain might be learning its connections. The reason for that is that, as far as I know, neuroscientists are really convinced that the brain cannot implement backpropagation because the signals in the synapses are only moving one direction. So if you have a neuroscience motivation and you want to say, 'Okay, how can I come up with something that tries to approximate the good properties of backpropagation without doing backpropagation?' That's what the forward-forward algorithm is trying to do. But if you are trying to just engineer a good system, there is no reason to not use backpropagation. It's the only algorithm, really.
turns.zh: 我认为这是在没有反向传播的情况下训练神经网络的一次尝试，我认为如果你有动力去理解大脑是如何学习其连接的，这尤其有趣。原因是，据我所知，神经科学家们确信大脑无法实现反向传播，因为突触中的信号只朝一个方向移动。所以如果你有神经科学的动机，你想说，‘好吧，我如何能提出一种在不进行反向传播的情况下近似反向传播良好特性的方法？’这就是前向-前向算法试图做的。但如果你只是想工程化一个好的系统，没有理由不使用反向传播。它真的是唯一的算法。

ts.sec: Humans as an Example of Intelligence

turns.en: I've heard you in different contexts talk about the need, like using humans as the existing example case that AGI exists, right? So at what point do you take the metaphor less seriously and feel don't feel the need to pursue it in terms of research? Because it is important to you as a sort of existence case. Like at what point do I stop caring about humans as an existence case of intelligence, or as the example of the model you want to follow in terms of pursuing intelligence in models?
turns.zh: 我在不同场合听你谈到过这种需要，比如把人类作为 AGI 存在的现有案例，对吧？那么你在什么时候会不再那么认真地对待这个比喻，觉得没有必要在研究上追求它？因为它对你来说作为一种存在案例很重要。比如在什么时候我不再关心人类作为智能的存在案例，或者作为你在模型中追求智能时想要遵循的模型范例？

turns.en: I see. I mean, I think it's good to be inspired by humans. I think it's good to be inspired by the brain. I think there is an art to being inspired by humans and the brain correctly, because it's very easy to latch on to a non-essential quality of humans or of the brain. And I think many people whose research is trying to be inspired by humans and by the brain often get a little bit specific, people get a little bit to, 'Okay, so what cognitive science model should we follow?' At the same time, consider the idea of the neural network itself, the idea of the artificial neuron. This too is inspired by the brain, but it turned out to be extremely fruitful. So how do you do this? What behaviors of human beings are essential that you say, 'This is something that proves to us that it's possible'? What is inessential? No, actually this is some emergent phenomenon of something more basic, and we just need to focus on getting our own basics right. I would say that one can and should be inspired by human intelligence, with care.
turns.zh: 我明白了。我的意思是，我认为受到人类的启发是好的。我认为受到大脑的启发是好的。我认为正确地受到人类和大脑的启发是一门艺术，因为很容易抓住人类或大脑的非本质特征。而且我认为许多试图从人类和大脑中寻找灵感的研究人员常常变得有点具体，人们会有点，‘好吧，那么我们应该遵循哪种认知科学模型？’同时，考虑神经网络本身的想法，人工神经元的概念。这也是受大脑启发的，但结果证明它非常富有成效。那么你如何做到这一点？人类行为的哪些本质特征让你说，‘这向我们证明了这是可能的’？哪些是非本质的？不，实际上这是更基本事物的某种涌现现象，我们只需要专注于把我们的基础做对。我会说，一个人可以并且应该受到人类智能的启发，但要小心。

ts.sec: Correlation Between Early Success and Continued Excellence

turns.en: Final question: why is there in your case such a strong correlation between being first to the deep learning revolution and still being one of the top researchers? You would think that the two things wouldn't be that correlated, but why is that correlation?
turns.zh: 最后一个问题：为什么在你的案例中，成为深度学习革命的第一批人之一和仍然是顶尖研究者之一之间有如此强的相关性？你会认为这两件事不会那么相关，但为什么会有这种相关性？

turns.en: I don't think those things are super correlated, indeed. I feel like in my case, I mean honestly it's hard to answer the question. You know, I just kept trying really hard, and it turned out to have sufficed thus far.
turns.zh: 我确实不认为这些事情是高度相关的。我觉得就我而言，老实说这个问题很难回答。你知道，我只是不断非常努力地尝试，结果到目前为止这已经足够了。

turns.en: Got it, so it's a perseverance.
turns.zh: 明白了，所以是坚持不懈。

turns.en: I think it's a necessary but not a sufficient condition. Like, you know, many things need to come together in order to really figure something out. You need to really go for it, and also need to have the right way of looking at things. And it's hard to give a really meaningful answer to this question.
turns.zh: 我认为这是一个必要但不充分的条件。你知道，很多事情需要结合起来才能真正弄清楚一些事情。你需要真正全力以赴，同时也需要有正确的看待事物的方式。很难对这个问题给出一个真正有意义的答案。

turns.en: Alright, Ilya, it has been a true pleasure. Thank you so much for coming out to the Society. I appreciate you bringing us to the offices.
turns.zh: 好了，Ilya，这真是一次愉快的经历。非常感谢你来到学会。感谢你带我们到办公室。

turns.en: Thank you, yeah. I really enjoyed it. Thank you very much.
turns.zh: 谢谢你，是的。我真的很享受。非常感谢。

turns.en: Hey everybody, I hope you enjoyed that episode. Just wanted to let you know that in order to help pay for the bills associated with this podcast, I'm turning on paid subscriptions on my Substack at warcpatel.com. No important content on this podcast will ever be paywalled, so please don't donate if you have to think twice before buying a cup of coffee. But if you have the means and you've enjoyed this podcast or gotten some kind of value out of it, I would really appreciate your support. As always, the most helpful thing you can do is to share the podcast, send it to people you think might enjoy it, put it on Twitter, your group chats, etc. Just spread the world. Appreciate you.
turns.zh: 大家好，希望你们喜欢这一集。只是想告诉你们，为了帮助支付这个播客的相关费用，我将在我的 Substack（warcpatel.com）上开启付费订阅。这个播客上的任何重要内容都不会被付费墙限制，所以如果你在买一杯咖啡之前都要三思，请不要捐款。但如果你有能力，并且喜欢这个播客或从中获得了某种价值，我将非常感激你的支持。一如既往，你能做的最有帮助的事情就是分享这个播客，把它发给你认为可能喜欢的人，放到 Twitter、群聊等地方。传播开来。感谢你们。

turns.en: Listening. I'll see you next time. Cheers.
turns.zh: 收听。下次见。干杯。

consensus.en: Next-token prediction can surpass human performance if the base neural net is smart enough.
consensus.zh: 如果基础神经网络足够智能，下一个词预测可以超越人类表现。

consensus.en: Alignment of models smarter than us is very difficult and requires multiple approaches.
consensus.zh: 对齐比我们更聪明的模型非常困难，需要多种方法。

consensus.en: Reliability is the key barrier to economic value from AI, not capability.
consensus.zh: 可靠性是AI产生经济价值的关键障碍，而非能力。

consensus.en: Scaling laws link next-token prediction accuracy to reasoning, but the link is complex.
consensus.zh: 缩放定律将下一个词预测准确性与推理联系起来，但这种联系很复杂。

consensus.en: Hardware is not a fundamental limitation; cost per flop is what matters.
consensus.zh: 硬件并非根本限制；每浮点运算的成本才是关键。

contrarian.en: Next-token prediction can extrapolate to hypothetical superhuman behavior, not just imitate.
contrarian.zh: 下一个词预测可以外推到假设的超人行为，而不仅仅是模仿。

contrarian.en: A mathematical definition of alignment is unlikely; multiple partial definitions are needed.
contrarian.zh: 对齐的数学定义不太可能；需要多个部分定义。

contrarian.en: Data for reinforcement learning is already mostly AI-generated, not human.
contrarian.zh: 强化学习的数据已经大部分由AI生成，而非人类。

contrarian.en: The current paradigm of next-token prediction may go very far, but not all the way to AGI.
contrarian.zh: 当前的下一个词预测范式可能走得很远，但并非直达AGI。

contrarian.en: Inference cost is not prohibitive if the model is useful enough, like a lawyer's fee.
contrarian.zh: 如果模型足够有用，推理成本并非高不可攀，就像律师费一样。
